answers.field_label: What Brings You To My Office Briefly Describe Your Current Situation
answers.value:
Over the past year, I’ve noticed my anxiety and low mood getting progressively worse to the point that it’s affecting my work, my relationship with my partner, and my ability to be present with my 6-year-old daughter. I’ve always been a bit of a worrier, but lately it feels like my mind never shuts off. I find myself constantly thinking about worst-case scenarios—losing my job, something happening to my daughter, my partner deciding he’s had enough of me being so on edge. 

I work as a project coordinator for a mid-sized tech company, and the job can be demanding with tight deadlines and frequent last-minute changes. Over the last several months I’ve started having trouble concentrating during meetings, and I sometimes reread the same email 3–4 times because I’m afraid I’ll miss something and mess up. I’ve started staying late at work and logging back in at night to double-check things, which means I’m not really mentally home even when I’m physically there. My manager hasn’t said anything formally, but she’s commented that I seem overwhelmed and more reactive than usual.

At home, my partner, Jason, has mentioned that I seem distant and irritable. Little things set me off—like if he forgets to take out the trash or if my daughter, Lily, drags her feet getting ready for school. I’ll snap or raise my voice and then immediately feel guilty and ashamed. There are days when I feel like I’m just going through the motions: work, dinner, bedtime routine, then collapsing on the couch scrolling on my phone because I don’t have the energy to do anything else. I used to enjoy reading novels, doing yoga, and baking on weekends, but I rarely do those things anymore. Even when I have the time, I feel too drained or unmotivated, like everything is just one more thing on a never-ending to-do list.

Physically, I’ve been having trouble sleeping. I either can’t fall asleep because my mind is racing with all the things I didn’t get done, or I wake up at 3 or 4 a.m. thinking about work or money and can’t fall back asleep. Some nights I get maybe 4–5 hours of broken sleep. I’ve also noticed that my appetite is irregular—some days I forget to eat until mid-afternoon, other days I find myself snacking mindlessly, especially on sweets, when I’m stressed. I’ve had a few episodes that I think might be mild panic attacks: my heart starts pounding, my chest feels tight, my hands get shaky, and I feel like I need to escape the situation. This has happened a couple of times in crowded grocery stores and once during a presentation at work. 

Emotionally, I feel like I’m on a roller coaster. I can be holding it together and then suddenly feel like I want to cry for no obvious reason. I’ve also been struggling with a lot of self-criticism—telling myself I’m a bad mom, not good enough at my job, and that everyone else seems to handle life better than I do. I don’t have thoughts of wanting to hurt myself, but I have had moments where I think, “If I could just disappear for a while, that would be easier.” That thought scares me and is part of why I’m reaching out now.

I’ve been thinking about therapy for a long time but kept putting it off because I told myself things weren’t “bad enough” and that I should be able to handle it on my own. Recently, after snapping at my daughter for spilling juice and seeing how scared she looked, I realized I don’t want this to be her memory of me. Jason gently suggested I talk to someone, and my primary care doctor also recommended therapy at my last visit when I mentioned how stressed and overwhelmed I’ve been feeling. I’m hoping to get help managing my anxiety and mood, to develop healthier coping strategies, and to feel more like myself again—more patient, more present, and less like I’m constantly bracing for something to go wrong.

answers.field_label: How Long Has This Been Occurring What Do You Believe Caused It What Have You Tried To Change It
answers.value:
Looking back, I think I’ve had some level of anxiety since I was a teenager, but it became noticeably worse after my daughter was born six years ago. At that time I chalked it up to being a new mom and sleep-deprived, and some of it did ease up after the first year. The more intense combination of anxiety and low mood that I’m feeling now has been going on for about 10–12 months, with the last 4–6 months being especially difficult.

There were a few specific stressors that seemed to pile up around the same time. At work, our department went through a restructuring about a year ago, and I ended up taking on more responsibilities without much additional support. I’m now managing two large projects instead of one, and there’s more pressure to “do more with less.” Around that same time, my mom’s health started to decline—she has Type 2 diabetes and some mobility issues—and although she lives about 45 minutes away, I’ve been trying to help her coordinate appointments and manage her medications. I feel caught in the middle between caring for my mom, being there for my daughter, and keeping up at work. I think this “sandwich” feeling has been a big contributor.

On top of that, Jason and I had a rough patch in our relationship last fall. We weren’t communicating well, arguing more about money and household responsibilities, and I often felt resentful that I was carrying more of the mental load (scheduling, planning, remembering everything). Even though things between us have improved somewhat in the last couple of months, I don’t think I ever really processed how stressed and hurt I felt during that time. Instead, I just kept pushing through and telling myself to be grateful that we have jobs and a healthy child, which then made me feel guilty for struggling at all.

I also grew up in a family where emotions weren’t really talked about. My dad had issues with alcohol when I was younger and could be unpredictable, so I learned to be hyper-vigilant—always scanning the environment and trying to keep the peace. I suspect some of my current anxiety is tied to that old pattern of constantly anticipating what might go wrong and feeling responsible for preventing it. Now, when something small goes off-plan (like a deadline shifting or my daughter getting sick), I react as if it’s a much bigger threat, and my body goes into overdrive.

Over the past year, I’ve tried several things on my own to cope. I’ve read self-help books and articles about anxiety and burnout, and I’ve tried to implement some of the advice, like setting boundaries at work and practicing gratitude. I downloaded a meditation app and used it fairly regularly for a couple of months; it helped a bit with falling asleep, but I had trouble sticking with it once my schedule got busier. I also tried to start jogging again, which I used to do in my twenties, but I ended up feeling discouraged when I couldn’t keep up the routine more than a couple of times a week.

I’ve made some practical changes, like limiting coffee to one cup in the morning instead of drinking it all day, and trying to keep a more consistent bedtime. I’ve experimented with making lists to organize my tasks and using a planner to schedule time for myself, but what happens is that when I get overwhelmed, those systems fall apart and I end up feeling even more behind. A few months ago, I spoke with my primary care physician about my symptoms—trouble sleeping, feeling on edge, frequent worry—and she suggested therapy and mentioned that medication might be an option if things didn’t improve. I was hesitant to start medication without first trying therapy, partly because of stigma and partly because I worry about becoming “dependent” on something.

Socially, I’ve pulled back from some friendships because I feel like I’m always complaining or tired, and I don’t want to be a burden. I’ve tried to push myself to say yes to social invitations occasionally—like a monthly book club—but I often cancel at the last minute because I feel too drained. When I do go, I usually feel somewhat better afterward, which tells me that isolation is probably making things worse, but in the moment it’s hard to override the urge to just stay home.

Despite all these attempts, I still feel like I’m operating in a constant state of tension and exhaustion. The worry and self-criticism have become louder, and my usual strategies (lists, exercise, occasional meditation) aren’t enough anymore. That’s why I’m seeking therapy now—I’ve reached the point where I recognize that I need more structured, professional support to understand what’s going on, break some of these patterns, and learn healthier, more sustainable ways to cope, rather than just white-knuckling my way through each day.

answers.field_label: Who Lives In Your Home With You
answers.value:
I currently live in a two-bedroom apartment with my long-term partner, Jason Miller (age 39, software engineer, together for 9 years), and our daughter, Lily (age 6, first grade). We also have a 4-year-old mixed-breed rescue dog named Bailey who is very much part of the family. Jason and I share a bedroom, and Lily has her own room. We have joint financial responsibility for rent and utilities, though I typically handle most of the day-to-day planning (scheduling appointments, school forms, groceries, etc.).

My mom, Karen (age 67), does not live with us but stays over occasionally on weekends when she has early medical appointments nearby. She lives about a 45-minute drive away in Salem, Oregon, and I’m her main point of contact and support person, but she maintains her own apartment. No other adults or children live in the home with us full-time.

answers.field_label: How Would You Describe Your Current Relationship
answers.value:
Jason and I have been together for about nine years and living together for eight. Overall, our relationship is loving and stable, but it has definitely been under strain over the last year. We are both pretty introverted and tend to avoid conflict, which means that small resentments sometimes build up until they spill over in unproductive ways.

On the positive side, Jason is very supportive in many practical ways. He helps with school drop-offs and pick-ups when his schedule allows, cooks dinner a few nights a week, and is very involved with our daughter—reading to her at night, helping with homework, and taking her to the park on weekends. We share similar values around parenting, finances (we’re both fairly cautious with money), and we both care about creating a stable, calm home environment, especially given that we each had somewhat chaotic childhoods. We enjoy some of the same hobbies, like watching movies, going for walks, and trying new coffee shops when we have childcare.

Where we struggle is mostly in communication and emotional intimacy. When I get anxious or overwhelmed, I tend to withdraw or become irritable, and he tends to shut down or retreat into his work or hobbies (like video games). This can create a cycle where I feel abandoned or like I’m carrying everything alone, and he feels criticized or like he can’t do anything right. We’ve had recurring disagreements about division of household labor and the “mental load”—I often feel like I’m the one keeping track of all the details (doctor’s appointments, school events, family birthdays, etc.), while he feels like his contributions (like working late to meet deadlines, doing dishes, and fixing things around the house) are overlooked.

Over the last few months, we’ve made some effort to be more intentional about connecting. We’ve started having short “check-in” conversations on Sunday evenings to go over the upcoming week, and occasionally we’ll have an at-home date night after Lily goes to bed (ordering takeout and watching a movie together without our phones). These moments remind me that we do care about each other and want the relationship to work. Jason has been generally supportive of me seeking therapy—he’s said he’s open to doing couples counseling at some point if that would help, although we’re starting with individual therapy for me first.

I would describe our current relationship as fundamentally solid but strained by stress, miscommunication, and my own mental health struggles. There is still affection, shared history, and a desire to make things better, but we need better tools for handling conflict, sharing responsibilities more fairly, and staying emotionally connected instead of slipping into parallel lives under the same roof.
